note.soap:
S: A 35-year-old female presents with a 5-day history of burning urination. She reports no fever, no flank pain, and no vaginal discharge. Patient is not pregnant. There are no reported allergies.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Vital signs and physical findings are not available. No laboratory or imaging results are available at this time.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Uncomplicated urinary tract infection (cystitis)  
  - Differential: Urethritis, vaginitis, interstitial cystitis, early pyelonephritis (less likely given no fever or flank pain)  
  - Red flags considered: Fever, flank pain, vaginal discharge, pregnancy—all denied or not present.

P:  
  - Disposition: Manage as uncomplicated UTI in urgent care setting with follow-up in primary care if symptoms worsen or persist beyond treatment.  
  - Plan: Empiric antibiotic therapy with nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days, assuming no contraindications; alternatively, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole if local resistance patterns allow and no allergies. Recommend increased fluid intake and symptomatic relief with phenazopyridine if needed. Advise to seek in-person evaluation if fever, flank pain, or worsening symptoms develop.  
  - Safety net: Return for urgent evaluation if fever, flank pain, vomiting, or persistent symptoms beyond 48 hours after starting antibiotics. If symptoms worsen or new symptoms develop, patient should be seen in person for further assessment and possible urine studies.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- Duration: 5 days  
- Fever: no  
- Flank pain: no  
- Discharge: no  
- Pregnancy: no  
- Sex at birth: female
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
